architecture.summary: Chain-of-Verification (CoVe) is a prompting-based method that uses a single large language model to reduce hallucinations through a four-step deliberation process: (1) generate an initial response, (2) plan verification questions to fact-check the response, (3) answer those questions independently (in joint, 2-step, or factored variants with varying context isolation), and (4) generate a final verified response incorporating the verification results. The method uses only text-based prompting with few-shot demonstrations; no external tools, training, or model modification occurs.

biblio.title: Chain-of-Verification Reduces Hallucination in Large Language Models

claims.statement: CoVe more than doubles precision on the Wikidata list-based task, improving from 0.17 to 0.36 (2-step variant)
CoVe reduces hallucinated (negative) entities on Wikidata from 2.95 to 0.68 while maintaining positive entities at 0.59 to 0.38
CoVe achieves 23% improvement in F1 on MultiSpanQA closed-book QA (factored variant: 0.48 vs few-shot baseline 0.39)
CoVe achieves 28% improvement in FactScore on longform biography generation (factor+revise variant: 71.4 vs few-shot baseline 55.9)
CoVe factor+revise outperforms ChatGPT (FactScore 58.7) and PerplexityAI (61.6) on biography generation with FactScore 71.4
When querying individual entities via verification questions, 70% are correctly answered compared to only 17% in the original list-based question baseline

Factored and 2-step CoVe variants consistently outperform joint CoVe across all tasks because preventing the model from attending to the original baseline response reduces repetition of hallucinations
While CoVe reduces hallucinations, it does not remove them completely, meaning the method can still generate incorrect or misleading information even after improvement over baseline
